# Partner Institutions

NANOGrav partner institutions include a diverse range of institutions throughout North America and across the globe

##### NANOGrav Institutions

## The International [Pulsar](/glossary/pulsar "A rapidly rotating neutron star emitting electromagnetic radiation in a beam which rotates with the neutron star, causing pulsed energy received at the earth at the rotation period of the neutron star. ") Timing Array

NANOGrav shares the goal of detecting and studying gravitational waves using pulsars with several other international collaborations. The European Pulsar Timing Array (EPTA) is using several radio telescopes located throughout Europe, the Indian Pulsar Timing Array (InPTA) is using the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope, located in Pune, India, and the Parkes Pulsar Timing Array (PPTA) is using the Parkes Telescope, located in New South Wales, Australia. All four members make up the International Pulsar Timing Array (IPTA), with additional collaborators in South Africa and China. IPTA members agree to share data and strategies and to work together collaboratively with the goal of detecting gravitational waves more quickly. Through the IRES and AccelNet programs, NANOGrav members are also able to study abroad with members of the IPTA. The IPTA holds yearly meetings, with each consortium taking a turn to host on a rotating basis.

The map below shows EPTA, InPTA, and PPTA member institutions. Scroll around the map and click on a marker to see the contact information for an IPTA member at that institution.

URL: https://www.ligo.caltech.edu/page/observatories-collaborations

SUMMARY:
An even larger gravitational-wave observatory collaboration exists between LIGO's detector network and other detectors and researchers around the world.

FULL CONTENT:
![Ligo_signals](/assets/ligo_signals-c600800835440c1e3722874dc29012d2.png)
![Llo_full_aerial_2023](/system/pages/images/22/page/LLO_Full_Aerial_2023.jpg?1711675083)

LIGO Livingston Interferometer. (Credit: Caltech/MIT/LIGO Lab)

# Global Collaboration

The U.S. National Science Foundation Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory (NSF LIGO) is not the only gravitational-wave observatory in the world. While NSF LIGO's two detectors work together, an even larger gravitational-wave observatory [collaboration](https://dcc.ligo.org/public/0190/M2300188/004/LSC_Program_2023.pdf) exists between LIGO's detector network and other detectors and researchers around the world, including [Virgo](https://www.virgo-gw.eu/) in Italy and [KAGRA](https://gwcenter.icrr.u-tokyo.ac.jp/en/) in Japan. Data from LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA are combined and analyzed jointly. This collaboration significantly enhances the search for and confirmation of gravitational waves.

To learn more about LIGO's broader, global gravitational wave research collaboration, click on the links below:

--- SOURCE 3: International Gravitational Wave Observatory Network ---
URL: https://igwn.docs.ligo.org/web/website/

SUMMARY:
Members of the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA Collaboration - the IGWN Design Committee - started developing a Charter and Bylaws for IGWN in early 2024.

FULL CONTENT:
![logo](assets/images/logo.png)
![logo](assets/images/logo.png)

# About IGWN[¶](#about-igwn "Permanent link")

The International Gravitational-Wave Observatory Network (IGWN) is envisioned as a self-governing consortium using gravitational-wave (GW) interferometric detectors to explore the fundamental physics of gravity and observe the universe. It aims to advance the frontiers of GW astronomy, astrophysics, and fundamental physics through the development, commissioning, and operation of the IGWN ground-based interferometric gravitational-wave detectors; through the development and deployment of techniques for gravitational-wave observation; and through interpretation of gravitational-wave data.

Once fully established, IGWN will comprise the existing large-scale ground-based gravitational-wave observatories and the worldwide consortium that carries out its scientific mission. IGWN will build upon the success of the current generation of GW collaborations – the LIGO Scientific Collaboration (LSC), the Virgo Collaboration (VC), and the KAGRA Collaboration (KC) – evolving them into a single unified global consortium that executes its scientific program in a coordinated and efficient fashion.

Members of the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA Collaboration - the IGWN Design Committee - started developing a Charter and Bylaws for IGWN in early 2024. Those draft documents envision an IGWN Board that provides strategic leadership in developing and implementing IGWN's program of activities to achieve its goals and future plans. The Inaugural IGWN Board (IIB) was established in January 2025 as the first step in the formation of IGWN. The IIB has its members:

The IIB anticipates the release of the initial IGWN Charter, Bylaws and Program in October 2025. The IIB will invite existing LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA groups to join IGWN in early 2026. Other elements of IGWN will then be established with the intention of IGWN being fully operational in early 2027. Applications from other groups will be considered after this date.

## Topic [Radio Astronomy](/science/topics/radio-astronomy)

We observe with the largest telescopes in the world in order to detect electromagnetic waves with the very longest wavelengths. Radio astronomy allows us to probe energetic processes which are invisible to optical telescopes.

![Artist Rendering of a Pulsar Timing Array with a Gravitational Wave Background](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20xmlns%3D'http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F2000%2Fsvg'%20viewBox%3D'0%200%201%201'%2F%3E)

## Topic [15-Year Publications' Summaries](/15yr/Summary)

## Collaboration

Our goal is to detect low-frequency gravitational waves and use our measurements to characterize the low-frequency gravitational wave universe. Our [pulsar](/glossary/pulsar "A rapidly rotating neutron star emitting electromagnetic radiation in a beam which rotates with the neutron star, causing pulsed energy received at the earth at the rotation period of the neutron star. ") timing observations also allow a wealth of other astrophysical experiments and applications.

--- SOURCE 5: Our Collaborations | LIGO Lab ---
URL: https://www.ligo.caltech.edu/page/ligo-scientific-collaboration

SUMMARY:
In turn, the Collaboration contributes significantly to the Lab's mission of developing gravitational-wave detectors, running the Observatories, and generally

FULL CONTENT:
![Ligo_signals](/assets/ligo_signals-c600800835440c1e3722874dc29012d2.png)

# Our Collaborations

### *The LIGO Scientific Collaboration*

![LSC Logo long](https://www.ligo.caltech.edu/system/media_files/binaries/718/large/LSC_snip.png?1661213251 "LSC Logo long")

The U.S. National Science Foundation Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory (NSF LIGO) Laboratory is the largest member of the [LIGO Scientific Collaboration (LSC)](http://www.ligo.org). Established in 1997, the LSC includes over 1200 scientists from over 100 institutions in 18 different countries. The LSC carries out the science of the two LIGO detectors in Louisiana and Washington State, and that of the GEO600 detector in Hannover, Germany. It is organized around three general areas of research: analysis of NSF LIGO data searching for gravitational waves from astrophysical sources, detector operations and characterization, and development of future large-scale gravitational-wave detectors.

As the largest member of the LSC, the NSF LIGO Laboratory plays a special role in the Collaboration, and members of the LSC play special roles in the LIGO Lab. For example, the LSC Spokesperson is a member of the LIGO Laboratory Directorate, and the Directorate is responsible for the Collaboration's scientific direction. The LIGO Lab participates fully in the Collaboration, holding (by election or by appointment) a number positions of leadership of Collaboration entities, and the Lab follows Collaboration rules for publications, presentations, and in sharing LIGO data with the greater scientific community. In turn, the Collaboration contributes significantly to the Lab’s mission of developing gravitational-wave detectors, running the Observatories, and generally furthering the field. For further information on the LSC and how to [join](http://www.ligo.org/about/join.php) the Collaboration, see the [LSC website](http://www.ligo.org/). See the current [list of LSC institutions](https://sites.google.com/ligo.org/ligo-scientific-collaboration/home) and the number of scientists working at each.

Graduate students and some undergraduates work within the Collaboration to engage in research for their degrees while contributing original science to the Collaboration. Many of these students continue on as scientists at the LIGO Laboratory or the other institutions that are members of the LSC, or take their unique, LSC-acquired skillsets in lasers, optics, materials science, engineering and more to industry, contributing to some of the world's most innovative technologies.

### *The LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) Collaboration*

![Virgo KAGRA logo](https://www.ligo.caltech.edu/system/media_files/binaries/721/large/Virgo_Kagra.png?1661212844 "Virgo KAGRA logo")

LIGO's ties with the Virgo gravitational wave detector in Italy and the KAGRA detector in Japan make up the global "LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA Collaboration"

The nature of gravitational-wave astronomy makes cooperation and collaboration between the detectors essential. Combining the data from multiple gravitational-wave detectors increases our confidence in the signals we detect, increases sensitivity to weaker or more distant sources, and leads to more precise measurements of the source parameters, in particular, the sky position. In addition to the twin LIGO detectors, two other large-scale Michelson interferometers in the world are currently sensitive to gravitational-waves: Virgo and KAGRA. The [Virgo detector](https://www.virgo-gw.eu/), a 3-km interferometer in Cascina, Italy, is operated by the European Gravitational Observatory and funded by INFN (Italy), CNRS (France) and Nikhef (Netherlands). The [KAGRA observatory](https://gwcenter.icrr.u-tokyo.ac.jp/en/) is an underground 3-km interferometer in Kamioka, Japan. It is operated by ICRR, KEK and NAOJ and funded by MEXT (Japan). The development, building, and operation of Virgo and KAGRA are managed by the [Virgo Collaboration](https://www.virgo-gw.eu/about/scientific-collaboration/) and the [KAGRA Collaboration](https://gwcenter.icrr.u-tokyo.ac.jp/en/organization), respectively.

The LSC and the Virgo Collaboration have been carrying out joint analyses of available data sets and co-authoring observational result papers since 2010. Beginning in 2021, the KAGRA Collaboration also began co-authoring observational results from the second half of LIGO's third observing run, O3. Together, these three entities comprise the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA Collaboration (LVK Collaboration). For information on how to cite papers by the LVK Collaboration (or the earlier LIGO-Virgo Collaboration), please see the PDF in: [Brief Guide to Citing the LIGO–Virgo–KAGRA Collaboration](https://dcc.ligo.org/LIGO-M2200155/main/public).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Gravitational wave astronomy relies on a global network, including LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA observatories, enhancing detection capabilities through coordinated international efforts.
Collaborations like the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA facilitate joint research initiatives, allowing scientists to combine expertise and resources for groundbreaking discoveries in gravitational wave detection.
International collaborations establish data sharing agreements, enabling observatories to share critical observational data, enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of gravitational wave research.

Collaborative publications among institutions foster a unified scientific narrative, showcasing joint findings and advancements in gravitational wave astronomy, thereby increasing credibility and visibility.

International collaborations significantly impact the global science community by pooling resources, enhancing research capabilities, and accelerating advancements in understanding the universe through gravitational waves.
